articles.title: How I Built a Neural Model from Scratch, Running Entirely in the Browser
articles.excerpt: Yesterday I took the stage at the Codecon Meetup here in Curitiba to share a story that started as a fun experiment and turned into a real product. A story about constraints, curiosity, and a few other things.
articles.body:
## Educbank•1 thousand followers

Educbank•1 thousand followers

Yesterday, I took the stage at the Codecon Meetup here in Curitiba to tell a story that started as a joke and turned into a real product. A story about constraints, curiosity, and about 3,500 lines of TypeScript that perform machine learning without leaving the browser.

It was my first time presenting this project to a live audience, and I must say: the energy of the dev community is something else. Spot-on questions, laughter at the right moments, and that genuine exchange that only happens in person.

I want to share here a summary of what I presented, for those who weren't there and for those who were and want to review.

## It all started in a Hack Lab

In 2025, I participated in a Hack Lab at Codecon. The challenge: 4 hours, handcrafted code, zero AI, and the theme was to prove that remote work works.

In 30 minutes, I sketched on the whiteboard the MVP of a Chrome extension that monitored your tabs and gave you a "procrastination index." Like a scoreboard: you spent 47% of your time on productive sites and 53% procrastinating. Congratulations, you're losing.

The fun part was the persona. The extension built a defense narrative for the dev, as if a lawyer were translating their browsing data for the manager. "My client spent 3 hours on Stack Overflow, Your Honor. That's not procrastination, it's intensive research."

And thus, Saul Goodman was born. The sarcastic and persuasive lawyer from the series Better Call Saul, except instead of defending criminals, he defends you... from yourself.

After the Hack Lab, the project didn't die. It evolved through 6 milestones until it became what it is today: a complete ML model running 100% in the browser.

## The problem I needed to solve

The idea was clear: automatically classify sites as productive or procrastination, all within a Chrome Extension. It seems simple until you remember the constraints.

In Manifest V3, Google Chrome killed persistent background pages. Now you have a Service Worker that the browser can kill at any moment. No reliable WebGL, no WebGPU, no persistent process.

What I couldn't use:

TensorFlow (500KB+, unstable WebGL in Service Workers), ONNX Runtime (2MB of WASM), external API (privacy is a central commitment).

- TensorFlow (500KB+, unstable WebGL in Service Workers),

- ONNX Runtime (2MB of WASM),

- external API (privacy is a central commitment).

What I had:

TypeScript with Float32Array, IndexedDB, 50ms budget per classification ~10MB of memory.

- TypeScript with Float32Array,

- IndexedDB,

- 50ms budget per classification

- ~10MB of memory.

> The decision was: implement everything from scratch. Forward pass, backpropagation, optimizer, feature engineering, calibration, statistical validation. All in TypeScript.

## The architecture: Wide & Deep in the browser

The model is inspired by the paper "Wide & Deep Learning for Recommender Systems" by Google (Cheng et al., 2016), the one Google used for recommendations in Google Play. I took the architecture and adapted it to run in pure TypeScript with sparse vectors.

The intuition is simple. There are two types of knowledge:

Memorization: "github.com is productive. Period." I know because I've seen this site 200 times and the user confirmed. Generalization: "sites that have a code editor, high interaction rate, and are accessed during work hours tend to be productive." I've never seen this specific site, but it looks like productive sites.

- Memorization: "github.com is productive. Period." I know because I've seen this site 200 times and the user confirmed.

- Generalization: "sites that have a code editor, high interaction rate, and are accessed during work hours tend to be productive." I've never seen this specific site, but it looks like productive sites.

The Wide branch does the first: linear regression over 131 thousand dimensions. Fast, memorizes direct patterns. The Deep branch does the second: embeddings of dimension 8, a hidden layer of 16 neurons with ReLU, and learns nonlinear combinations of features.

> The trick: the loop only traverses the active features. A typical page has 30 to 80 active features, not 131 thousand. In practice, the forward pass takes less than 5 milliseconds.

482 lines of TypeScript. No framework. Forward pass, backpropagation, L2 regularization, gradient clipping, AdaGrad. All in a single file.

## Features: where the intelligence comes from

The model exists, but garbage in, garbage out. The quality of the features is what separates a useful model from an academic one.

There are 3 layers of signals:

The first layer is the content of the page. The content script looks at the DOM and extracts categorical features like host, title, headings, editor flags, schemas. The tokenization is Unicode-aware because Saul Goodman supports 13 languages. The second layer is behavior over 14 days. And here lies the magic. The same domain (youtube.com, for example) can be productive or procrastination. It depends on how you interact. If you spend 20 minutes on a tutorial video, with interactions, without skipping: productive. If you're jumping from shorts to shorts: procrastination. The model learns this. The third layer consists of natural signals. 16 continuous signals inspired by the paper "The Cost of Interrupted Work: More Speed and Stress" (Mark et al. 2008), which showed that after each interruption, it takes us an average of 23 minutes to regain focus. I measure things like: how many times you switched tabs in the last 10 minutes, what the continuity of your focus is, how long it takes to return to a domain. This dramatically changes the prediction.

- The first layer is the content of the page. The content script looks at the DOM and extracts categorical features like host, title, headings, editor flags, schemas. The tokenization is Unicode-aware because Saul Goodman supports 13 languages.

- The second layer is behavior over 14 days. And here lies the magic. The same domain (youtube.com, for example) can be productive or procrastination. It depends on how you interact. If you spend 20 minutes on a tutorial video, with interactions, without skipping: productive. If you're jumping from shorts to shorts: procrastination. The model learns this.

- The third layer consists of natural signals. 16 continuous signals inspired by the paper "The Cost of Interrupted Work: More Speed and Stress" (Mark et al. 2008), which showed that after each interruption, it takes us an average of 23 minutes to regain focus. I measure things like: how many times you switched tabs in the last 10 minutes, what the continuity of your focus is, how long it takes to return to a domain. This dramatically changes the prediction.

## From strings to numbers: feature hashing

Alright, I have features like "host:github.com" and "flag:editor." But the model needs numbers. How to transform arbitrary strings into numerical indices?

The classic solution is to maintain a dictionary (feature goes to index), but the dictionary grows infinitely and any new feature becomes out-of-vocabulary.

The solution I used was Feature Hashing (Weinberger et al., 2009). It takes the feature string, applies the FNV-1a hash (ultra-fast, non-cryptographic), and the result modulo 131,072 becomes the index. The least significant bit of the hash defines a signal: +1 or -1. Features that collide at the same index will have opposite signals, and by the law of large numbers, the contributions cancel each other out. This is called signed hashing.

The result: fixed memory of ~512KB, zero dynamic allocation, O(|features|) complexity per prediction. No vocabulary, no OOV, no headache with serialization.

## Calibration and validation: the rigor that makes the difference

There is a problem that almost every ML project in production faces and few people talk about: "–The model says it has 90% certainty, but it only gets it right 70% of the time." This has a name: overconfidence. The paper "On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks" (Guo et al. 2017) showed this very clearly.

In Saul Goodman, I solved it with Temperature Scaling. It's a single scalar parameter T that divides the score before the sigmoid. The accuracy remains the same, but the confidence starts to reflect reality. If the model says 80%, it really gets it right 80%. It seems like a detail, but when the user sees the confidence percentage on the screen, it needs to be honest. Otherwise, they stop trusting and uninstall.

To measure if the calibration is working, I use the Expected Calibration Error (ECE) with 10 bins. Before calibration, the ECE was around 0.12. After Temperature Scaling, it dropped to 0.03. And T is recalibrated at each validation cycle because browsing behavior changes, and calibration degrades silently if it stays static.

But there's another point that I consider even more important: how to know if the model is ready to make decisions on its own?

> Imagine day 1. The user just installed the extension. The model has zero data. If it starts classifying everything aggressively and makes a mistake right away, that's it. The user loses trust and uninstalls.

That's why I created a Validation Gate. The model starts in conservative mode, only classifying when it has very high certainty. And it only relaxes the thresholds when it passes 5 simultaneous criteria: sufficient samples, improvement in false positive rate, stable precision, confidence interval via bootstrap with 1,000 iterations, and McNemar test p-value < 0.05.

## Recommended by LinkedIn

The bootstrap gives me a distribution, not a single number. I generate 1,000 resamples with replacement and look at the 95% confidence interval. If the lower bound is already better than the previous threshold, then I relax it. I don't look at the best case, I look at the reasonable worst case. And the McNemar test complements: it compares predictions pairwise and tests if the difference is statistically significant.

In practice, the Validation Gate takes on average 3 to 5 days to release the model for autonomous mode. It seems slow, but that's exactly the point. In the first days, the model asks for more explicit feedback, and each feedback is high-quality data. When it finally relaxes, it does so with confidence.

The model doesn't relax because "it seems better." It relaxes because it has statistical proof that it improved.

## How the model improves on its own

The model predicts, the Validation Gate ensures that it is good. But how does it improve with real use, without asking the user to label things all day?

Two strategies.

The first is the Review Queue, based on the Active Learning survey (Settles 2009). If human feedback is expensive, I need to ask for examples where the model has the most to learn. There are two priorities: borderline (the probability is at 0.05 of the threshold, the model almost decided but couldn't) and uncertainty (probability close to 0.5, maximum uncertainty). A queue of at most 20 candidates. Each click from the user is worth gold. The second is pseudo-labels. The model generates automatic labels for domains where it has very high confidence (>= 0.93 or <= 0.07), but with 7 layers of protection. Quarantine if the user has already contradicted, cooldown of 12 hours per domain, maximum of 20 pseudo-labels per day, 3 consistent predictions in 7 days, no conflict with recent explicit feedback, and FPR cannot be worse than baseline. And the weight of a pseudo-label is 0.1 against 1.0 of explicit feedback. The model learns 10 times slower with auto-generated data.

- The first is the Review Queue, based on the Active Learning survey (Settles 2009). If human feedback is expensive, I need to ask for examples where the model has the most to learn. There are two priorities: borderline (the probability is at 0.05 of the threshold, the model almost decided but couldn't) and uncertainty (probability close to 0.5, maximum uncertainty). A queue of at most 20 candidates. Each click from the user is worth gold.

- The second is pseudo-labels. The model generates automatic labels for domains where it has very high confidence (>= 0.93 or <= 0.07), but with 7 layers of protection. Quarantine if the user has already contradicted, cooldown of 12 hours per domain, maximum of 20 pseudo-labels per day, 3 consistent predictions in 7 days, no conflict with recent explicit feedback, and FPR cannot be worse than baseline. And the weight of a pseudo-label is 0.1 against 1.0 of explicit feedback. The model learns 10 times slower with auto-generated data.

The quarantine layer, for example, I added after the model stubbornly believed that a site was productive even after the user corrected it 3 times.

## Explainability: the model needs to justify itself

The model decides, but if the user doesn't understand why, they won't trust it. And if they don't trust it, they won't use it. And if they don't use it, the model doesn't receive feedback and doesn't improve. It's a cycle.

So each suggestion comes with explanations. For each active feature, I calculate the contribution: the weight of the wide branch times the hash signal times the feature value. I sort by absolute contribution and select in a balanced way: up to 2 natural signals, up to 2 behavioral, up to 2 content. Plus 1 counter-argument, which is the strongest opposing evidence.

This is not an LLM generating text. Each signal has a fixed template mapped by feature key. "Usage history outside of hours in this domain favors productivity." "Intent ambiguity observed on the page favors procrastination." Deterministic, fast, no hallucination.

The user sees the classification, the confidence, the reasons for and against, and buttons to confirm or correct. Total transparency.

## All together: the end-to-end pipeline

To give a concrete sense of how everything connects, I will describe what happens behind the scenes when you visit github.com/pulls.

The Content Script collects metadata from the DOM. The ML Suggestion Engine runs everything in a single synchronous call: logs the behavior event, aggregates 14 days of history, extracts features, builds natural signals, vectorizes via FNV-1a, forward pass in less than 5ms, calibrates with Temperature Scaling, classifies, generates explanations, and tries to generate pseudo-labels if it's confident. A toast appears: "github.com seems productive. Confirm?" If the user confirms, it runs the update SGD online, recalibrates if necessary, reevaluates the Validation Gate, and persists everything in IndexedDB.

The storage uses two IndexedDB databases with limits: 25 thousand training examples, 2 thousand for calibration, 2 thousand for testing. Retention of 30 days. When full, it uses reservoir sampling.

And the split between training, calibration, and testing is deterministic. I take the FNV-1a hash of the domain, modulo 100. This ensures that the same domain is always in the same split. No data leakage.

> Zero network requests. No auxiliary workers. Synchronous.

## What I learned in practice

These are lessons I wish I had heard before starting. Each one came from a real bug, from hours of debugging, from things that broke in production.

Float32 is not Float64. This seems obvious, but it's not. Without gradient clipping and without checks for Number.isFinite() on every multiplication, after 48 hours of real use, NaN starts to appear. And the worst part: NaN is silent. The model doesn't crash. It simply stops learning, and you only notice days later.

AdaGrad beats Adam when you have memory constraints and sparse features. Adam needs 3 arrays per parameter (it would triple memory from 9MB to 27MB). AdaGrad needs only 1, and for sparse features, it has a natural property: rare features keep the learning rate high, and frequent features slow down on their own.

Temperature Scaling works with few samples. I started with Platt Scaling (2 parameters). It overfit with few data. I switched to Temperature (1 parameter). It converges quickly, is stable, and with 25 samples, it already calibrates well.

And determinism is debugability. Fixed LCG seed for weight initialization, FNV-1a for splits. Reproducible model across machines. When a user reports a bug, I can reproduce it exactly.

## Constraints are liberating

This was the message I used to close the talk and I want to repeat it here because I truly believe in it.

When you can't use TensorFlow, can't use a server, can't send data outside, you are forced to understand every piece of the system. And when you truly understand what's behind a forward pass, an optimizer, a calibration, you make better decisions. Even when you later go back to using frameworks.

To give a sense of the scale: Saul Goodman today has approximately 3,500 lines of ML code, distributed across 13 files, with zero external machine learning dependencies. There are about 2.2 million parameters, 9.5MB in runtime, forward pass in less than 5ms and backprop in less than 15ms. All running in your browser, without sending a single byte to any server.

## Want to see the details?

The complete presentation of the talk, with all diagrams and technical details, is available here: https://gamma.app/docs/Como-Construi-um-Modelo-Neural-do-Zero-no-Browser-0a9ll6hyg5wyeyt

Saul Goodman is open source and is on the Chrome Web Store. If you want to test it, install it, or just browse the code:

Chrome Web Store: https://chromewebstore.google.com/detail/fllkkpfaajgppbnlfoebeendceckcffe/

GitHub: https://github.com/Donotavio/saul_goodman

If you liked it, install it, test it, and let me know what you think. And if you want to contribute, open an issue, or just take a look at the architecture, the repository is open. All contributions are welcome.

Thanks to Codecon for once again opening space for the community to share knowledge. And thanks to everyone who was there yesterday and made this talk an incredible experience.

Articles:

1. Cheng et al. (2016) — Wide & Deep Learning for Recommender https://arxiv.org/abs/1606.07792 2. Guo et al. (2017) — On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.04599 3. Mark et al. (2008) — The Cost of Interrupted Work: More Speed and Stress https://ics.uci.edu/~gmark/chi08-mark.pdf 4. Weinberger et al. (2009) — Feature Hashing for Large Scale Multitask Learning https://arxiv.org/abs/0902.2206 5. Settles (2009) — Active Learning Literature Survey https://burrsettles.com/pub/settles.activelearning.pdf

articles.title: The Model That Recommends Apps for 1 Billion People, and Why It’s Still Relevant in 2026
articles.excerpt: Imagine the following scenario. You are an engineer at Google, it's 2015, and you have a problem on your hands: the Play Store has over 1 million apps and more than 1 billion active users.
articles.body:
Imagine the following scenario.

You are an engineer at Google, it's 2015, and you have a problem on your hands: the Play Store has over 1 million apps and more than 1 billion active users. Your recommendation system needs to decide, in milliseconds, which apps to show to each person.

The team tries logistic regression with crossed features. It works well for known patterns: those who installed Spotify are likely interested in Deezer. But it fails miserably when a new app appears. No history, no recommendation.

The team tries deep neural networks. The embeddings generalize to new apps, but they start recommending nonsensical things, like a cooking app for someone who only plays FPS. The network "learns" patterns that do not exist.

Two paths. Each solves half of the problem. Each fails on the other half.

What if the answer wasn't to choose between them?

In 2016, Heng-Tze Cheng and a team of researchers at Google published "Wide & Deep Learning for Recommender Systems." A paper that proposed something that, in retrospect, seems obvious: to unite the two paths into a single end-to-end trained model.

The model went straight to production. In the Google Play Store. Serving recommendations for over 1 billion active users.

The result in A/B test: +3.9% in app acquisition rate compared to the previous model.

At a billion scale, that means millions of additional installations. Per day.

## The Trade-off That the Entire Industry Accepted as Inevitable

To understand why this paper is important, one must understand the dilemma that the RecSys community faced (and that many professionals still face).

Memorization is when the model learns patterns directly from the data: "users who did X also did Y." It is precise, interpretable, reliable. But it has a ceiling: it only works for what has already been seen.

Generalization is when the model extrapolates to the unknown: "this new app has similar characteristics to apps that this user profile likes." It is powerful, but risky; it can extrapolate too much and recommend garbage.

The industry treated this as a binary trade-off. Either you built a robust linear model with manual feature engineering (memorization), or you invested in deep learning with embeddings (generalization).

The central insight of the paper was to reject this false dichotomy.

## The Architecture: Two Systems, One Brain

The Wide & Deep combines two components trained jointly, and this detail is crucial.

## Wide: The Component That Remembers

A linear model that receives raw features and cross-product transformations:

> AND(user_installed_app=spotify, impression_app=youtube_music) = 1

This crossed feature tells the model, explicitly: "when these two conditions are true simultaneously, pay attention." No abstraction. No hidden layers. Pure and surgical memorization.

The wide component is optimized with FTRL (Follow-the-regularized-leader) with L1 regularization, which naturally produces a sparse model. Only the features that truly matter survive. Elegant.

## Deep: The Component That Generalizes

A feed-forward neural network where high cardinality categorical features (app_id, user_id) are transformed into dense embeddings of 32 dimensions, concatenated with continuous features and passed through 3 hidden layers (1024, 512, 256 units, ReLU).

The embeddings are the heart of generalization. An app that has just been published on the Play Store has no installation history. But its embedding captures that it is a productivity app, for Android 12+, with a minimalist interface. That is already enough for the model to position it in the latent space close to apps that a particular user profile already knows and likes.

## Joint Training: Where the Magic Happens

The final prediction combines the two:

P(Y=1|x) = σ(w_wide · [x, φ(x)] + w_deep · a_final + b)

But the key point is not the formula; it's that the gradients flow to both components simultaneously.

This is not an ensemble. In an ensemble, each model is trained in isolation and the results are combined afterward. Here, the wide component "knows" that the deep exists and adapts to complement it. The wide focuses on exceptions and business rules. The deep focuses on general patterns. Each specializes in what it does best because it is aware of the other.

This difference between joint training and ensemble is subtle, but it is what separates a good model from a model that works in the Play Store.

## Recommended by LinkedIn

## The Pipeline That Serves 1 Billion Users

The paper is not just about model architecture. It describes the complete production pipeline, and that is what makes it rare in the literature.

Retrieval: For each query, a set of ~100 candidate apps is retrieved by a combination of ML models and heuristics. Ranking: The Wide & Deep ranks each candidate by the probability of installation. Serving: The results are served in ~10ms per request.

- Retrieval: For each query, a set of ~100 candidate apps is retrieved by a combination of ML models and heuristics.

- Ranking: The Wide & Deep ranks each candidate by the probability of installation.

- Serving: The results are served in ~10ms per request.

The model was trained with over 500 billion examples. The features include installation history, demographic data, app characteristics, device context, and impression data.

One technical detail that I find particularly elegant: new training data is incorporated via warm-starting. The model is not retrained from scratch each cycle. The existing embeddings and weights are preserved as initialization, and only the deltas are learned. This is what allows updating a model of this scale with enough speed to keep up with the pace of new app releases.

## Why This Matters in 2026, and More Than You Imagine

I could argue that Wide & Deep is relevant only for historical reasons. But that would be dishonest. The reality is that the principles of this paper are more present today than ever. They just changed their clothes.

## The DNA of Wide & Deep Is Everywhere

The models that came afterward, such as DeepFM (2017), DCN (Deep & Cross Network) (2017), DCN v2 (2020), and Facebook's DLRM (2019), are all variations on the same theme: combining explicit feature interactions with learned deep representations. Wide & Deep was not just the first. It defined the architectural paradigm that the entire RecSys industry has adopted over the last decade.

When you see a recommendation on YouTube, Instagram, TikTok, or Amazon, behind it lies an architecture that descends, directly or indirectly, from what this paper proposed.

## RAG Is Wide & Deep for LLMs

Here’s a connection that I rarely see being made.

The RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) pattern that dominates LLM applications today is, conceptually, the same principle of Wide & Deep applied to text.

The retrieval (fetching relevant documents) is memorization: direct access to specific and factual information. The LLM (generating responses based on context) is generalization: extrapolating, synthesizing, connecting.

RAG works because it combines the two. Just like Wide & Deep, it is not retrieval OR generation. It is retrieval AND generation, trained (or integrated) to complement each other.

The 2016 paper already gave us the mental template to understand why RAG works. The industry took 7 years to reach the same conclusion in another domain.

## The Lesson That Became More Important Over Time

In 2026, with models of hundreds of billions of parameters, the temptation is to solve everything with more compute. More layers. More data. More parameters.

Wide & Deep is living proof that the right architecture matters more than brute force. Two relatively simple components, combined intelligently, outperformed larger and more complex models. In production, at scale, with measurable results.

This principle has not aged. It has become more urgent.

## What I Take From This Paper to My Work

After studying and applying RecSys principles in different contexts, some lessons from this paper have become almost axioms in how I think about model design:

Linear models are not primitive; they are precise. Logistic regression with crossed features is not an "old" technique. It is a tool that captures signals that deep networks simply ignore. Combining is almost always better than replacing.

Cross-product features are the feature engineering that still works. Before adding architectural complexity, explicitly code the interactions that you know exist. The wide component of the paper does exactly that, and it is half the reason for its success.

Joint training > ensemble. If your components are complementary, train them together. The specialization that emerges from joint training cannot be replicated by later combination.

The pipeline is the product. The model is one piece. Retrieval, serving, warm-starting, monitoring: the paper dedicates as much space to the pipeline as to the architecture. This is not an accident. In production, the system around the model often matters more than the model itself.

## To Close

Wide & Deep Learning is not just a 2016 paper. It is the architecture that still recommends apps to 1 billion people on the Google Play Store. It is the ancestor of all modern RecSys architecture. And its principles (combining memorization with generalization, training jointly, prioritizing architecture over brute force) are more relevant today than when they were published.

If you work with machine learning and have not yet read this paper, you are building on a foundation you do not know.

It's worth the read.

Reference: Cheng, H.T. et al. (2016). Wide & Deep Learning for Recommender Systems. Proceedings of the 1st Workshop on Deep Learning for Recommender Systems (DLRS 2016). arXiv:1606.07792

Which classic ML paper do you think should be discussed more? Comment here. I want to create a series on the papers that shaped the industry.

articles.title: How to Structure and Make a Data Team Truly Work!
articles.excerpt: In a world where quick and accurate decisions make all the difference, data teams have ceased to be a luxury and have become an essential engine of competitiveness. It's not just about collecting data or building beautiful dashboards.
articles.body:
## Educbank•1 thousand followers

Educbank•1 thousand followers

In a world where quick and accurate decisions make all the difference, data teams have ceased to be a luxury and have become an essential engine of competitiveness. It's not just about collecting data or building beautiful dashboards. It's about creating a sustainable flow of transforming data into real value for the business. In this journey, three profiles emerge with complementary roles: Data Engineers, Data Analysts, and Data Scientists. Understanding how each contributes, and especially how they collaborate, is crucial for creating a structure that generates lasting impact.

## The Foundation:

> Why is a data team strategic?

Before we talk about roles, it's important to understand why seriously investing in a data team is a strategic step. Data, in isolation, is worthless. It's the ability to translate this data into information, knowledge, and ultimately wisdom, that transforms companies. This chain is known as the DIKW pyramid, which shows how raw data evolves into intelligent decisions when well-structured and interpreted. Without a dedicated team, organizations end up with fragmented projects, lack of governance, and analyses that never impact strategic decisions. This is where the need to combine people, processes, and technology comes in.

## The roles that make up an effective team:

## 1. Data Engineer

> The architect of the infrastructure!

Data Engineers are the ones who build and maintain the organization's data backbone. They not only extract data from different sources but also ensure that this flow operates reliably, scalably, and with the quality standards that other areas require. A robust pipeline means less rework, less firefighting, and more focus on generating value. Key points of the role:

Design and implement data ingestion and transformation pipelines.Ensure that data is placed in reliable, versioned, and traceable formats for consumers.Scale the architecture as the company grows and needs evolve.

- Design and implement data ingestion and transformation pipelines.

- Ensure that data is placed in reliable, versioned, and traceable formats for consumers.

- Scale the architecture as the company grows and needs evolve.

Instead of simply "running jobs", a good engineer thinks about robustness, automation, and governance from the start.

## 2. Data Analyst

> Translator between numbers and business.

Data Analysts are the ones who transform numbers into useful narratives for business areas. They stay close to real everyday questions: "Why did the churn rate increase?", "Which customer segments are growing the most?", "Which KPIs are out of standard?" This role is essential because this is where data stops being technical and becomes useful and understandable for managers and stakeholders. Typical contributions:

Building dashboards and reports that explain phenomena.Interpreting data in light of strategic objectives.Defining and tracking metrics that tell a story for the business.

- Building dashboards and reports that explain phenomena.

- Interpreting data in light of strategic objectives.

- Defining and tracking metrics that tell a story for the business.

An excellent analyst not only masters SQL and BI tools but also knows how to tell stories that guide real decisions.

## 3. Data Scientist

## Recommended by LinkedIn

> Explorer and modeler of future scenarios.

While the analyst answers "what happened and why", the data scientist seeks to understand what might happen and how to act on it. This role is often associated with Machine Learning techniques, advanced statistics, and predictive modeling. But it goes far beyond that; a good data scientist translates business problems into testable hypotheses and models that add continuous value.

Main activities:

Building and validating predictive or prescriptive models.Deep exploration of patterns that are not visible in descriptive analyses.Collaborating with engineers to put models into production.

- Building and validating predictive or prescriptive models.

- Deep exploration of patterns that are not visible in descriptive analyses.

- Collaborating with engineers to put models into production.

The great differentiator of this role is the ability to anticipate scenarios, something that, when done well, transforms into a competitive advantage.

## How do these roles interconnect in practice?

There is no real data team without collaboration. Data engineers should not work in the dark but aligned with what analysts and scientists need: reliable, available, and understandable data. On the other hand, analysts and scientists cannot expect their work to be efficient if they spend time cleaning data or rebuilding pipelines all the time. The ideal model follows a continuous cycle:

Clear definition of the business problem.Always coming from the area that demands results.Data base engineering.Pipelines that ensure quality and governance.Exploratory analysis and reports.Transforming data into immediate insights.Advanced and predictive modeling.Anticipating future possibilities.Feedback and iteration.Refining both the data and the questions.

- Clear definition of the business problem.Always coming from the area that demands results.

- Data base engineering.Pipelines that ensure quality and governance.

- Exploratory analysis and reports.Transforming data into immediate insights.

- Advanced and predictive modeling.Anticipating future possibilities.

- Feedback and iteration.Refining both the data and the questions.

## Common organizational structures and when to adopt them

In practice, three organizational models have gained traction:

Centralized.The entire data team is in a single area, serving the whole company. This tends to strengthen governance and reduce duplication of efforts.Decentralized.Specialists are embedded in product or business teams, favoring speed but requiring discipline to avoid silos.Hybrid.Centralized engineering with analysts and scientists close to business areas. Many organizations seen as mature end up in this model because it combines governance and agility.

- Centralized.The entire data team is in a single area, serving the whole company. This tends to strengthen governance and reduce duplication of efforts.

- Decentralized.Specialists are embedded in product or business teams, favoring speed but requiring discipline to avoid silos.

- Hybrid.Centralized engineering with analysts and scientists close to business areas. Many organizations seen as mature end up in this model because it combines governance and agility.

> There is no "one size fits all"! The choice depends on the maturity of the data and the strategic objectives of the organization.

## Culture and practices that make a data team thrive

In addition to roles and structures, some practices that stand out in the market as real differentiators are:

Aligning success metrics with business outcomes and not with the number of reports delivered.Documenting pipelines, metrics, and data definitions to avoid misunderstandings and rework.Promoting fluid communication between technical teams and business areas.Investing in governance from day one with data contracts, glossary, and clear SLAs.

- Aligning success metrics with business outcomes and not with the number of reports delivered.

- Documenting pipelines, metrics, and data definitions to avoid misunderstandings and rework.

- Promoting fluid communication between technical teams and business areas.

- Investing in governance from day one with data contracts, glossary, and clear SLAs.

## Conclusion

A well-structured data team is much more than titles in the organization. It is a combination of complementary roles. Engineering that delivers reliability, analysis that generates meaning, and science that anticipates the future. All driven by a data-oriented culture and continuous collaboration.

articles.title: When JSON Broke Us — and How the “VARIANT” Type in Databricks Saved Us.
articles.excerpt: “In a pipeline, a JSON brought a new and silent field — metadata.new_feature — and out of nowhere, several downstream jobs simply failed.
articles.body:
## Educbank•1 thousand followers

Educbank•1 thousand followers

> "In a pipeline, a JSON brought a new and silent field —metadata.new_feature — and out of nowhere, several downstream jobs simply failed. That’s when we realized: our approach with JSON string could no longer handle evolutions."

## Introduction / motivation.

In the real world of data engineering, we don’t always control the source of the data. Upstream systems send JSONs with variable fields, new features appear without warning, and we need to absorb this without breaking the downstream ecosystem.

For a long time, our simple strategy was: we captured the raw JSON as a string, and then we paired it when necessary. It seemed reasonable — until it became an emergency routine every time the schema varied.

In this article, I will share how we adopted the VARIANT type within Databricks / Delta Lake, how the migration went, the trade-offs we faced, and the gains that made the team lighter.

## The collapse of the pipeline and the invisible tension.

To illustrate, imagine this scenario (based on what we went through):

The upstream registration system starts sending a new field called tracking.sessionId in the JSONs. In the staging environment, everything passes — there is no data with this field. In production, a real JSON includes tracking.sessionId, and our job that did SELECT json_data:sessionId AS session_id fails with a parsing error. The team rushes to add columns, adjust UDFs, emergency ALTER TABLE scripts, test, revalidate. Meanwhile, dashboards become unstable. With each new customer feature, we repeat the same cycle.

- The upstream registration system starts sending a new field called tracking.sessionId in the JSONs.

- In the staging environment, everything passes — there is no data with this field.

- In production, a real JSON includes tracking.sessionId, and our job that did SELECT json_data:sessionId AS session_id fails with a parsing error.

- The team rushes to add columns, adjust UDFs, emergency ALTER TABLE scripts, test, revalidate.

- Meanwhile, dashboards become unstable. With each new customer feature, we repeat the same cycle.

The tension between the software engineer and the data engineer was latent:

The software engineer wanted compression, smaller payload, to avoid sending superfluous fields. I, as a data engineer, wanted visibility, to capture all available data for future analytical uses.

- The software engineer wanted compression, smaller payload, to avoid sending superfluous fields.

- I, as a data engineer, wanted visibility, to capture all available data for future analytical uses.

We tried hybrid approaches — extracting columns for the most used fields, leaving a "residual JSON" field as a string — but the maintenance cost, conditional parsing, UDFs, and schema evolution was growing exponentially.

## The turning point: the discovery of the VARIANT type

In one of our architecture discussions, the mention of the VARIANT datatype in Delta Lake came up. I confess I had never used this feature, but upon researching, I realized it was exactly what we needed to deal with the unpredictability of MongoDB data.

## What is the VARIANT type?

The VARIANT is a data type designed to store semi-structured information — such as JSON, structs, arrays, maps, or even scalar values — in a single column, without the requirement of a fixed schema.

## Internal representation.

Instead of keeping the JSON as a string and requiring constant parsing, the VARIANT uses an internal binary format. This allows for more efficient navigation through the fields, reducing processing overhead and increasing performance in queries.

## Accessing fields.

The syntax for accessing subfields is quite natural. We can use paths like col:field, col:field.subfield or even specific functions like variant_get and try_variant_get to safely extract values.

## Inspecting content.

Another strong point is the ability to inspect the structure of the stored payload. Functions like schema_of_variant and schema_of_variant_agg allow for dynamic inference of the internal schema of a dataset, facilitating exploratory analyses and decisions on which fields to "promote" to explicit columns.

## Resource maturity.

It is worth noting that support for VARIANT is still in Public Preview, available from Databricks Runtime 15.3. This means that while it is fully functional, it may be subject to adjustments and improvements in future versions.

## Limitations to consider.

At the same time, the VARIANT type does not solve everything. It brings restrictions that need to be known before going into production.

Partitioning and clustering. VARIANT type columns cannot be used to partition tables, nor as clustering or Z-order keys. This means you cannot directly use the content of a VARIANT as a physical table organization strategy in Delta Lake. Databricks Documentation

Limited operations. It is not possible to apply GROUP BY, ORDER BY, DISTINCT, or set operators (UNION, INTERSECT) directly on VARIANT columns. When such operations are necessary, the solution is to extract the desired field to an explicit column before aggregation or sorting. Microsoft Learn.

Missing statistics. Delta does not collect min and max statistics on VARIANT columns. This can affect the optimizer's ability to apply efficient "data skipping" or pruning, impacting the performance of certain queries.

Maximum size. Each value stored in a VARIANT column has a limit of approximately 16 MiB. If a JSON document exceeds this size, it will be treated as a corrupted record.

Compatibility and protocol. When enabling VARIANT support in a table, the Delta protocol is elevated. This means that clients or tools that do not understand this new protocol (such as older versions of libraries) may no longer be able to read or write to the table.

Even with these limitations, the balance was positive in our experience: we gained flexibility without losing performance and significantly reduced the manual effort of the team to deal with the constant evolution of JSON schemas.

## Migrating to VARIANT: a step-by-step guide.

Here is a migration template that can serve as a basis for you to adapt to your context:

## 1. Preparation and freezing of ingestion:

Stop or pause ingestion processes (streams, jobs) that write to the target table. Ensure that there are no concurrent writes during the migration.

- Stop or pause ingestion processes (streams, jobs) that write to the target table.

- Ensure that there are no concurrent writes during the migration.

## 2. Create intermediate table (staging).

Assuming your current schema has something like:

You can create a copy:

## Recommended by LinkedIn

## 3. Drop / rename the original table.

You can rename or drop the original table:

## 4. Create a new table with VARIANT column

If you want, you can enable VARIANT support in an existing table via:

but normally prefer to recreate. Microsoft Learning.

## 5. Copy data converting payload

This step converts the JSON string payload to VARIANT.

## Adjusting ingestion pipelines.

After creating the table with VARIANT type support, the next step was to adapt our ingestion pipelines — both in batch and streaming — for the data to be written in this format.

In the case of pipelines that still received JSON as a string, we applied the parse_json(...) function during the write process. This conversion ensures that the content is already persisted as VARIANT, avoiding the need for parsing in each query.

When we used Auto Loader, we took advantage of the singleVariantColumn option, which allows loading the entire JSON directly into a VARIANT type column, greatly simplifying ingestion and reducing the effort of defining dynamic schemas.

Additionally, in APIs and connectors that already deliver data in JSON format, we configured for the payload to be sent directly to the VARIANT field, eliminating intermediate transformations.

This change brought clarity to the process: instead of maintaining different ingestion strategies to deal with data variability, we centralized everything in VARIANT — gaining consistency, performance, and fewer points of failure.

## 7. Validate and test.

Re-run jobs, dashboards, ETFs, and downstream pipelines. Check:

If old queries that expected to extract fields from the string still work (now using :field or variant_get) If the performance is acceptable. If cases of "malformed" or very large JSONs are captured as corrupted records.

- If old queries that expected to extract fields from the string still work (now using :field or variant_get)

- If the performance is acceptable.

- If cases of "malformed" or very large JSONs are captured as corrupted records.

## 8. Cleanup.

After confirming that everything works, drop the staging or old table, clean up metadata, backup if any.

This process is based on reports from users who have already adopted VARIANT. Medium+1

## Examples of use / queries with the new model.

Here are some examples that you can copy and adapt:

It is also important to work with schema_of_variant or schema_of_variant_agg:

These functions help understand the heterogeneity of the data and decide which fields to "promote" to explicit columns.

In the case of NULLs, note that there is a distinction between SQL NULL and variant NULL. Use is_variant_null(...) to differentiate. Databricks Documentation+1

## Expected results, gains, and traps we encountered.

## Gains we observed:

Less pipeline breakage when new fields arise — many new attributes are absorbed in the VARIANT payload without impacting transformations. Performance improvement in queries that previously parsed JSON repeatedly. In benchmarks published by Databricks, VARIANT outperformed JSON-string by ~8× in speed. Databricks Less effort in schema migration and maintenance of ALTER TABLE scripts. Greater freedom for upstream changes — the pipeline becomes more resilient. Ability to discover emerging fields via schema_of_variant, allowing informed decisions on which fields to extract.

- Less pipeline breakage when new fields arise — many new attributes are absorbed in the VARIANT payload without impacting transformations.

- Performance improvement in queries that previously parsed JSON repeatedly. In benchmarks published by Databricks, VARIANT outperformed JSON-string by ~8× in speed. Databricks

- Less effort in schema migration and maintenance of ALTER TABLE scripts.

- Greater freedom for upstream changes — the pipeline becomes more resilient.

- Ability to discover emerging fields via schema_of_variant, allowing informed decisions on which fields to extract.

## Traps and hard lessons:

Jobs that did GROUP BY payload:eventType needed to be rewritten, extracting the field beforehand, because VARIANT cannot be grouped directly. Legacy tools or clients that did not support the new table protocol began to fail. Loss of automatic statistics (min/max) for VARIANT columns affects pruning and read optimization. Very large JSON records (>16 MiB) were treated as corrupted — we had to deal with extreme cases. In cases where a field has different types in the rows (e.g., number vs string), there was a type conflict: schema_of_variant returned as VARIANT instead of STRING or INT. The limitations of GROUP BY / ORDER BY / DISTINCT require you to plan ahead which fields should be extracted from the payload to explicit columns.

- Jobs that did GROUP BY payload:eventType needed to be rewritten, extracting the field beforehand, because VARIANT cannot be grouped directly.

- Legacy tools or clients that did not support the new table protocol began to fail.

- Loss of automatic statistics (min/max) for VARIANT columns affects pruning and read optimization.

- Very large JSON records (>16 MiB) were treated as corrupted — we had to deal with extreme cases.

- In cases where a field has different types in the rows (e.g., number vs string), there was a type conflict: schema_of_variant returned as VARIANT instead of STRING or INT.

- The limitations of GROUP BY / ORDER BY / DISTINCT require you to plan ahead which fields should be extracted from the payload to explicit columns.

## Conclusion and recommendations for data teams.

This type of journey — from the predictable pain of JSON to the relief of VARIANT — brought us two central learnings:

Balance is essential. We did not extract everything to columns — only the fields that really matter for filters, aggregations, or joins — and left the rest in VARIANT. The hybrid model is powerful. Planning is key. Testing, controlled migration, compatibility with legacy clients, monitoring extreme payloads — all of this avoids surprises.

- Balance is essential. We did not extract everything to columns — only the fields that really matter for filters, aggregations, or joins — and left the rest in VARIANT. The hybrid model is powerful.

- Planning is key. Testing, controlled migration, compatibility with legacy clients, monitoring extreme payloads — all of this avoids surprises.

If I were to give a recommendation for those starting out:

Start with non-critical tables to experiment with VARIANT. Do comparative benchmarks JSON-string vs VARIANT. Monitor corrupted cases / large payloads. Use schema_of_variant_agg to understand which fields tend to appear. Plan incremental migrations: move to explicit columns only when truly necessary.

- Start with non-critical tables to experiment with VARIANT.

- Do comparative benchmarks JSON-string vs VARIANT.

- Monitor corrupted cases / large payloads.

- Use schema_of_variant_agg to understand which fields tend to appear.

- Plan incremental migrations: move to explicit columns only when truly necessary.

articles.title: Data Teams Are Not Bakeries.
articles.excerpt: The invisible cost of treating data like individual orders. Imagine walking into a bakery.
articles.body:
> The invisible cost of treating data as one-off requests.

Imagine walking into a pastry shop. A customer places a specific order, the cook rushes to fry it, delivers quickly, and soon another order arrives, completely different. There’s no standard recipe, no planning for the monthly menu, no business strategy behind it: just orders being fulfilled, one by one. This everyday scene is a perfect metaphor for what happens in many data teams within companies.

When we treat a data team like a pastry shop, the outcome is predictable: many orders coming in at the same time, all urgent, without context or strategic alignment. The team rushes to deliver, but quality varies, errors accumulate, and executives' trust in the data diminishes. The success metric shifts to “how many orders did we fulfill this week” instead of “how much strategic value did we generate for the business.”

This criticism is not new. Sven Balnojan, in What’s Wrong With Data Teams (Medium, 2022), shows how many BI teams end up becoming “internal ticket consultancies,” always ready to fulfill requests but rarely creating strategic impact. Yuliia Tkachova, in Why do data teams need to adopt product thinking? (Medium, 2023), reinforces that only by adopting a product mindset — with ownership, roadmap, and long-term vision — can teams break this cycle. Harvard Business Review also published, in A Better Way to Put Your Data to Work (2022), that companies need to treat data as a product to reduce redundancies and accelerate innovation.

In Brazil, the research State of Data Brazil 2024–2025 (Data Hackers + Bain) confirms this perception: a large part of teams still spends too much time on manual tasks and corrections instead of generating strategic insights. This reinforces the pastry shop feeling — a lot of effort in meeting immediate demand, little room for innovation.

The motivation behind this metaphor is clear: to provoke reflection. If executives continue to see data as something “over the counter,” the company will never reap the benefits of true digital transformation. The invisible costs are high: rework, fragility in processes, decisions based on unreliable information, and loss of motivation among professionals who feel like mere executors.

## Recommended by LinkedIn

But there are different paths. Magazine Luiza structured its culture and data platform to integrate physical and digital operations, supporting marketing, logistics, and expansion decisions (Ilumeo, 2020). Grant Thornton Brazil transformed HR data into People Analytics assets, consolidating records and anticipating strategic analyses (Mindsight, 2023). In these examples, teams stopped being order takers and became engines of strategy.

What differentiates these cases is the change in mindset. Instead of viewing data as one-off services, they began to treat it as strategic assets. This means creating clear roles — such as data product owners and data stewards — adopting prioritization criteria based on business value, establishing impact metrics, investing in governance and infrastructure. Above all, having executive support to sponsor the change.

Executives need to ask themselves: are we requesting “data pastries” to solve today’s one-off problems or are we building data assets that will sustain our business in the future? The difference between a pastry shop and a strategic kitchen is enormous. In the former, the rush never ends. In the latter, there is planning, quality, and long-term vision. And it is precisely this transition that will determine which companies will be able to compete in an increasingly data-driven world.

The roadmap was inspired by the demo 'Track 1'
articles.body:
> Summary: This article describes a practical architecture for automating data governance on Databricks using LLMs, Unity Catalog (governed tags, automatic classification, masks, and filters), AI Functions (ai_query), Vector Search, and Databricks Workflows. The roadmap was inspired by the demo “Track 1 – DAIWT SP 2025” and is ready for corporate adaptation focusing on LGPD/GDPR.

GitHub — Track 1 – DAIWT SP 2025 (https://github.com/databricks-solutions/databricks_demos_br/tree/main/daiwt_sp_2025/trilha1)

## 1) Context and Objectives

Effective governance in a Lakehouse requires classifying data, applying access policies, auditing usage, and measuring coverage— all continuously. LLMs reduce manual work: they can read data samples, metadata, and glossaries to suggest standardized tags, column masks, and row filters. In Databricks, this integrates natively with Unity Catalog and orchestration with Workflows.

The goal of this guide: to deliver a blueprint that you can reproduce to:

Automatically classify data (columns, tables) and assign tags (e.g.: privacy=PII, sensitivity=high). Apply access policies (masks/filters, or ABAC when enabled) based on these tags. Audit coverage and monitor quality/compliance. Do all of this in batches (daily/hourly) via Databricks Workflows.

- Automatically classify data (columns, tables) and assign tags (e.g.: privacy=PII, sensitivity=high).

- Apply access policies (masks/filters, or ABAC when enabled) based on these tags.

- Audit coverage and monitor quality/compliance.

- Do all of this in batches (daily/hourly) via Databricks Workflows.

## 2) Reference Architecture

Main Components

Unity Catalog (UC): unified catalog; supports governed tags, lineage, data classification via Databricks' AI engine; masks and filters. AI Functions (SQL): function ai_query() to invoke Model Serving endpoints (foundational or custom models) from SQL/Jobs. Vector Search (optional): for RAG of the governance agent (search in internal policies, glossaries, and LGPD standards). Databricks Workflows: scheduling and dependencies (YAML job or UI) to run the steps. System Tables/Information Schema: auditing of tags, grants, lineage, access.

- Unity Catalog (UC): unified catalog; supports governed tags, lineage, data classification via Databricks' AI engine; masks and filters.

- AI Functions (SQL): function ai_query() to invoke Model Serving endpoints (foundational or custom models) from SQL/Jobs.

- Vector Search (optional): for RAG of the governance agent (search in internal policies, glossaries, and LGPD standards).

- Databricks Workflows: scheduling and dependencies (YAML job or UI) to run the steps.

- System Tables/Information Schema: auditing of tags, grants, lineage, access.

High-Level Flow

Inventory → Sampling → LLM Classification → Normalization → Apply Tags → Policies → Audit & Metrics.

## 3) Prerequisites and Best Practices

Unity Catalog enabled and metastore configured. Privileges: APPLY TAG, USE CATALOG/SCHEMA, MODIFY on target tables/columns. Model Serving (foundational endpoint or custom model) accessible from the workspace. Serverless / Warehouse compatible for ai_query() (or execute on a cluster supported by the indicated Runtime). Defined tag standards: canonical keys and values (e.g.: privacy={PII,SENSITIVE,PUBLIC}, domain={financial,academic,...}). Corporate glossary (optional) in Vector Search to provide context to the LLM.

## 4) Automation Pipeline (Step by Step)

## 4.1 Object Inventory

## 4.2 Safe Sampling

## 6) Orchestration with Databricks Workflows (YAML)

Example of a YAML job to run daily at 02:00, using the same cluster (or serverless) and a notebook governance_llm_job.py in the project repository:

## 7) Observability and Governance KPIs

Tag coverage: % of tables/columns with defined privacy. Security gaps: PII columns without active mask/policy. Remediation time: median between detection and correction. Compliance by domain/squad: KPIs by owner/domain/system. Lineage-aware: prioritize widely disseminated upstreams.

Automate dashboards with System Tables and Information Schema.

## 8) Costs and Optimizations

Native automatic classification of UC can reduce costly scans. When using a custom LLM, apply sampling in batches. AI Functions: consolidate prompts, set temperature=0, reuse UDFs; evaluate token costs. Vector Search (if using RAG): choose optimized endpoints for storage to reduce cost per GB and speed up indexing. Agent Evaluation (optional): measure quality/cost/latency of the governance agent in batch.

- AI Functions: consolidate prompts, set temperature=0, reuse UDFs; evaluate token costs.

- Vector Search (if using RAG): choose optimized endpoints for storage to reduce cost per GB and speed up indexing.

- Agent Evaluation (optional): measure quality/cost/latency of the governance agent in batch.

## 9) Security, LGPD, and Compliance

Minimize personal data in sampling; prefer hashing/partialization. Legal basis & purpose as tags; principle of necessity for retention. Audit access and record decisions (Commits/PRs in Repos) for audit trail. This is not legal advice— validate with the privacy/legal team.

- Audit access and record decisions (Commits/PRs in Repos) for audit trail.

## 10) Advanced Extensions

RAG for internal policies: index regulations and data standards in Vector Search so that the LLM cites the corporate reference when proposing tags/policies. Integration with DBT: synchronize DBT selectors/tags with UC tags after each deploy. ChatOps: expose an endpoint (Databricks Apps/Serving) for on-demand classification requests. ABAC (Beta): declarative masking/filtering policies oriented to hasTag() at the catalog/schema level.

- RAG for internal policies: index regulations and data standards in Vector Search so that the LLM cites the corporate reference when proposing tags/policies.

- Integration with DBT: synchronize DBT selectors/tags with UC tags after each deploy.

- ChatOps: expose an endpoint (Databricks Apps/Serving) for on-demand classification requests.

- ABAC (Beta): declarative masking/filtering policies oriented to hasTag() at the catalog/schema level.

## 11) Appendix A — Quick SQL Examples

## Conclusion

With just a few native components from Databricks, you can close the governance cycle with LLM: inventory, classify, apply policies, and audit — all automated, reproducible, and measurable. Adjust the granularity (column/table), refine the prompt, and promote the use of governed tags as a common language among engineering, security, and legal.

articles.title: Prejudice in the Use of AI!
articles.excerpt: Prejudice in the use of Artificial Intelligence (AI), also known as algorithmic bias, refers to the tendency of AI systems to produce discriminatory outcomes due to human biases present in the training data or in the design of the algorithm. This issue is crucial, as AI systems
articles.body:
Prejudice in the use of Artificial Intelligence (AI), also known as algorithmic bias, refers to the tendency of AI systems to produce discriminatory results due to human biases present in the training data or the design of the algorithm. This issue is crucial, as biased AI systems can lead to unfair decisions, discrimination, and the perpetuation of social inequalities, impacting areas such as hiring, criminal justice, and healthcare. The relevance of this topic lies in its ability to amplify existing social problems, making a multidisciplinary approach essential for mitigation.

## Types of Bias in AI

Biases in AI can be categorized in various ways, based on their origins and impacts. Below, we present a classification based on recent research:

Data Bias: Occurs when the data used to train the AI is unrepresentative or contains historical biases. For example, if the training data for a facial recognition system predominantly includes faces of white people, the system may perform poorly for other ethnicities.
Algorithmic Bias: Refers to biases introduced by the algorithm itself or by the choice of model, such as the selection of features that favor certain groups. This can happen, for example, in credit granting algorithms that prioritize variables correlated with socioeconomic privileges.
Emergent Bias: Arises from the interaction between the system and its users, reflecting social dynamics that were not present in the initial data.
Evaluation Bias: Occurs when the model validation does not consider diverse groups, masking biases in specific subgroups.

- Data Bias: Occurs when the data used to train the AI is unrepresentative or contains historical biases. For example, if the training data for a facial recognition system predominantly includes faces of white people, the system may perform poorly for other ethnicities.

- Algorithmic Bias: Refers to biases introduced by the algorithm itself or by the choice of model, such as the selection of features that favor certain groups. This can happen, for example, in credit granting algorithms that prioritize variables correlated with socioeconomic privileges.

- Emergent Bias: Arises from the interaction between the system and its users, reflecting social dynamics that were not present in the initial data.

- Evaluation Bias: Occurs when the model validation does not consider diverse groups, masking biases in specific subgroups.

These categories help identify where and how bias can infiltrate the AI lifecycle, from problem definition to implementation.

## Practical Examples and Consequences

Real-world examples illustrate how bias in AI can have significant impacts. A notable case is Amazon's hiring algorithm, developed in 2014, which was discontinued in 2015 after it was found to discriminate against female candidates, reflecting historical hiring patterns that were predominantly male (Amazon’s AI Recruiting Tool). Another example is the facial recognition system, which often shows a higher error rate for darker-skinned individuals due to underrepresentation in training datasets (Face Recognition Bias).

The consequences are broad:

Social: Perpetuates stereotypes and discrimination, deepening racial and gender inequalities.

- Social: Perpetuates stereotypes and discrimination, deepening racial and gender inequalities.

Economic: Results in unfair hiring and credit scoring practices, limiting opportunities for marginalized groups.
Ethical: Violates principles of justice and equity, eroding public trust in AI technologies.

A study from USC Viterbi School of Engineering found that up to 38.6% of the “facts” used by AI knowledge bases, such as GenericsKB, may be biased, highlighting the extent of the problem (USC Bias Study).

## Mitigation Strategies

Mitigating bias in AI requires a systematic approach at all stages of development and deployment. Below, we detail strategies based on recent research:

Diverse and Representative Data Collection: Ensure that training data includes a wide range of demographics, avoiding underrepresentation. For example, facial recognition datasets should include faces of various ethnicities and genders.
Bias Detection and Audit Tools: Use methods such as bias detection algorithms (e.g., AIF360, AIF360 Tool) to identify disparities in model outcomes.
Justice-Conscious Algorithms: Develop models that incorporate fairness metrics, such as equal opportunity or equalized odds, to reduce disparities in protected groups.
Transparency and Explainability: Make AI systems transparent, allowing decisions to be explained, helping to identify and correct biases. Explainability techniques, such as “model cards” and “data sheets,” are recommended (Model Cards).
Ethical Guidelines and Regulations: Establish ethical standards, such as those proposed by the High-Level Expert Group on AI of the EU, and regulations that require regular audits of AI systems (EU AI Ethics Guidelines).
Continuous Monitoring and Evaluation: Regularly assess AI performance across different groups, adjusting the model as necessary to avoid amplifying biases over time.

## Future Perspectives and Challenges

The future of combating bias in AI involves significant challenges, such as the lack of diverse data in many domains, the complexity of defining fairness in varied contexts (for example, definitions of fairness may differ among communities, as discussed in Justice Definitions), and the trade-off between accuracy and fairness. Future research should prioritize:

Development of robust methods for bias detection and correction, such as mitigation algorithms (Bias Mitigation Research).
Creation of standardized benchmarks to assess fairness, facilitating comparisons between systems.
Integration of fairness considerations throughout the AI lifecycle, from problem definition to deployment.

articles.title: A New Industrial Revolution and Its Impact on the Job Market
articles.excerpt: Introduction Did you know that by 2030, artificial intelligence (AI) could add up to $15.7 trillion to the global economy? That's more than the combined GDP of China and India, according to a study by PwC (https://www.pwc.
articles.body:
## Introduction

Did you know that by 2030, artificial intelligence (AI) could add up to $15.7 trillion to the global economy? That's more than the combined GDP of China and India, according to a study by PwC (https://www.pwc.com/gx/en/issues/artificial-intelligence/publications/artificial-intelligence-study.html). This impressive figure not only reflects the economic potential of AI but also points to a radical transformation in the job market and the global economy — a change that echoes the great industrial revolutions of the past. Just as the steam engine propelled the First Industrial Revolution, AI is redefining the present and future of work. However, unlike previous revolutions, this new era brings unique speed, scale, and challenges that we cannot ignore.

## A New Industrial Revolution

Industrial revolutions have been pivotal moments in history. The first brought mechanization, the second introduced electricity and mass production, and the third gave us automation and computing. Now, we are living what many call the fourth industrial revolution, driven by AI and digitalization. Just like in past eras, we see machines replacing human tasks — but this time, not only physical tasks but also cognitive ones.

What sets this revolution apart is the accelerated pace. While previous transformations took decades to solidify, AI is advancing exponentially, reshaping markets in just a few years. An example of this is the adoption of generative AI: ChatGPT, for instance, reached 100 million users in less than a year, surpassing the adoption speed of technologies like smartphones and tablets, according to data from eMarketer AI Adoption (https://www.emarketer.com/content/generative-ai-adoption-climbed-faster-than-smartphones-tablets). Sectors such as healthcare, finance, manufacturing, and services are already being impacted, with algorithms optimizing processes, personalizing experiences, and even making complex decisions.

## Recommended by LinkedIn

## Challenges and Opportunities

Changes of this magnitude bring significant challenges. One of the most urgent is the reskilling of the workforce. The IMF estimates that nearly 40% of global employment is exposed to AI, with up to 60% of jobs in advanced economies potentially affected International Monetary Fund AI Economy Impact (https://www.imf.org/en/Blogs/Articles/2024/01/14/ai-will-transform-the-global-economy-lets-make-sure-it-benefits-humanity). This means that professions that once seemed secure, such as data analysis or even medicine, now face automation. A report from the McKinsey Global Institute suggests that up to 375 million workers may need to change occupations by 2030, while the World Economic Forum projects the creation of 97 million new jobs and the elimination of 85 million by 2025 Shaping the Future of Work, a WEF Global Shaper Initiative (https://www.weforum.org/reports/the-future-of-jobs-report-2018). This requires a swift response in terms of education and training, focusing on skills that complement AI, such as critical thinking and creativity.

Moreover, ethical issues — such as biases in algorithms and data privacy — require attention. A study from Harvard University highlights that AI systems can replicate social biases, such as in hiring decisions or criminal justice, due to biased training data Harvard University AI Ethics (https://news.harvard.edu/gazette/story/2020/10/ethical-concerns-mount-as-ai-takes-bigger-decision-making-role/). Regulation, such as the GDPR in Europe, seeks to mitigate these risks, but the debate on how to balance innovation and ethics continues.

On the other hand, the opportunities are enormous. AI can help solve complex global problems, such as climate change, by optimizing sustainable solutions, such as developing more efficient materials for wind turbines Worth AI Climate (https://worth.com/how-ai-can-tackle-5-global-challenges-2/). In healthcare, AI image analysis is already improving diagnostics, and in agriculture, autonomous machines are increasing efficiency and food security IABAC AI for Good (https://iabac.org/blog/ai-for-good-tackling-global-challenges-with-artificial-intelligence). For companies, strategically integrating AI is not just a matter of survival but a chance to innovate, create new business models, and capture emerging markets.

## Conclusion

As we navigate this new era, we need to embrace change with an open and proactive mindset. Investing in our development, collaborating across sectors, and ensuring that AI is used ethically and responsibly are fundamental steps. Just as the industrial revolutions of the past shaped the world we know, the era of AI will define the future — and it is in our hands, as professionals and leaders, to make this future a positive and inclusive one.

articles.title: The Pillar of Data Governance and Reliability!
articles.excerpt: If you work with data, you know that understanding where it comes from, how it is transformed, and where it goes is essential to ensure quality, reliability, and effective auditing. This is where data lineage comes in.
articles.body:
## Educbank•1k followers

Educbank•1k followers

If you work with data, you know that understanding where it comes from, how it is transformed, and where it goes is essential to ensure quality, reliability, and effective auditing. This is where data lineage comes in.

Imagine this scenario: a critical company report shows inconsistent numbers. Your team spends days trying to trace the source of the problem, analyzing various tables, pipelines, and transformations. In the end, they discover that a simple change in an upstream script impacted the final metric. If the data lineage had been well documented, the diagnosis would have taken minutes, not days.

## 🏆 Benefits of Data Lineage

1️⃣ Governance and Compliance— Regulations like LGPD and GDPR require data traceability. With well-structured lineage, responding to audits becomes simple and quick.

2️⃣ Debugging and Error Resolution— Bugs in ETLs and discrepancies in reports can be resolved more efficiently by understanding the entire data flow.

3️⃣ Performance Optimization— Identifying bottlenecks and eliminating unnecessary transformations improves pipeline efficiency and reduces computational costs.

4️⃣ Impact Analysis— Before modifying a pipeline or removing a table, it is crucial to know which processes will be affected.

## 🔍 Implementing Data Lineage in Databricks

The Unity Catalog provides a native approach to track and visualize data lineage in real-time within Databricks, allowing:

✅ To visualize the source and destination of data in tables, columns, and notebooks.

✅ To track which jobs, users, and processes accessed or modified the data.

## Recommended by LinkedIn

✅ Easily audit for compliance and security.

## 💡 Practical Example in Unity Catalog

Suppose your company has an ETL pipeline that reads raw data from a bucket in Azure Data Lake and transforms it into Delta tables. With the Unity Catalog, you can automatically visualize:

🔹 Which source fed the table (e.g., raw_data.customers)?

🔹 Which processes performed intermediate transformations?

🔹 Who accessed or altered the final data?

This reduces the risk of unforeseen impacts and accelerates decision-making based on reliable data.

## 🎯 Conclusion.

If you are a data professional, knowing and using data lineage is no longer optional. Tools like the Unity Catalog make this practice more accessible, efficient, and integrated into the Databricks ecosystem.

Want to improve data governance in your company? Start with lineage! 🔗💡

articles.title: The “PROMPT” Framework for AI and Its Benefits.
articles.excerpt: The PROMPT framework (Persona, Script, Objective, Model, Overview, Transform) was developed to guide the creation of clear and effective instructions for Artificial Intelligences. Below, I present each step, along with practical application examples.
articles.body:
The structurePROMPT(Persona, Script, Objective, Model, Overview, Transform) was developed to guide the creation of clear and effective instructions for Artificial Intelligences. Below, I present each step, along with practical application examples.

## 👨💻 Persona:

In this phase, we describe the role or identity that the AI should assume. For example:

> "You are an experienced marketing consultant with extensive knowledge in social media campaigns."

By defining the persona, we ensure that the tone of the response is appropriate for the requested role.

## 🎬 Script:

Here, we define the narrative structure or step-by-step of the content. Example:

> "I want you to create a script that includes an introduction, development, and conclusion, aimed at a blog post."

This guides the AI in organizing ideas, allowing for greater coherence.

## 🎯 Objective:

Determines the purpose of the request. Example:

> "Our goal is to engage the audience to subscribe to our email list."

With a clear objective, the AI can align each step of the content to achieve it.

## 👕 Model:

At this point, we define the output format. Example:

> "Structure the result in 5 short topics, with tips and practical examples in each one."

This helps the AI present the information clearly and organized.

## 🌍 Overview:

Provides the context and challenges of the target audience. Example:

> "The client is an entrepreneur who needs more reach on social media but is limited in budget."

This overview enriches the response with specific solutions for the presented scenario.

## 🌀 Transform:

Finally, we review and refine. It’s the time to suggest adjustments. Example:

> "Change the formal language to informal, include visual references, and add a call to action at the end."

This step ensures that the final response is polished and perfectly meets the desired outcome.

In my opinion, thePROMPToffers an effective guide for generating clear and objective instructions, enhancing the quality of responses provided by the AI. Following these six steps brings gains in clarity and cohesion, benefiting both creators and consumers of the content.
